speaker: interviewer
text: October 17th, 1996, interview with Jacob Wiener, tape number five. We were talking about your arrival in America and the outbreak of World War II in 1939. When you were in America, did you follow events in Europe closely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to communicate at all with family that was still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sent letters. I wrote to them. And I tried to find out what there was. Before I left Germany, many gave me passport pictures and other things, hoping that I could do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever hear from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them, I heard. Until, I think, 1940, 1941, I heard. But in 1941, all the correspondence with Germany was cut off. I never heard from them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you remain at that rabbinical college throughout the war, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remained there all the time till 1945, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the war was over, did, did you try to find relatives again in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to find people all the time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tried to [BACKROUND NOISE] talk to agencies in other places. But there were no lists available at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go when you graduated from rabbinical college?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I got a job as a youth counselor, psychologist, and in an, in an orphan home in Yonkers. Even in America, I was an enemy alien. And I needed permission to travel from New York, from Baltimore to Yonkers every week. And this was a place, there were about 180 young people there, boys-- only boys. The parents had sent these children beforehand. And they would come afterwards. And I stayed there for about one or two years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] While I was in the yeshiva, I also tried to do something about myself by-- and was an [INAUDIBLE] at one time. But then they told me, you are an enemy alien. You can't be an [INAUDIBLE]. Then I wanted to become a United States citizen, because I came to the United States on a non-quota visa as a student. I came here as a student. And I had to, I had to renew my permission every year in Washington. And one day when I wanted to become a citizen, I was called before a committee in Washington, where they had five different departments-- the Army, the Navy, the FBI. And other companies were there. It was at the height in [INAUDIBLE] 1943. And I was refused. But they said to me, come back in six months and reapply again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I did later on in 1945, the war was nearly over. I applied again. And then I said to the FBI, I am going to do translations for you. And OK. So I went to the Baltimore FBI in Baltimore. And they had me a little bit. And I became a citizen. At that time, you become a citizen. You get first papers. And five years later, you become a full citizens. So I became then a full citizen in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever work with bringing refugees over from Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I worked afterwords, first in the yeshiva and then afterwards in the teacher seminary for girls in Brooklyn. And I was put in charge of foreign students. And I worked with the State Department. And we brought over about 500 girls from Sweden, Hungary, Europe, and so forth, after the war. Some of them, I still know today.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you accomplish that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the institution had someone who was in Sweden. And from that point here, we had some contact. And from Hungary, we had some contact. And we needed, we needed a student visa. We needed a student-- they needed a student visa. They needed only-- the State Department only wanted to know whether they come over and we would provide them and take care of them, you see? And the seminary had a dormitory for girls. And so we took them over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you remain in New York then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in New York-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me think. At one point, I got a job in Mountain Dale, which is New York state in the Catskill Mountains, not far from Monticello. Then I lived there for two years. I stayed in Mountain Dale for two years. And they didn't have had-- they haven't had a school. I-- and I made a school there that still exists. So I stayed. And then I had jobs in Newark, New Jersey. And I went to Newark, New Jersey. And then when they came back, it's actually in Brooklyn. I came to Brooklyn to the [INAUDIBLE] teacher seminary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And afterwards, I, I made it. I got into the life insurance business. [LAUGHS]. Got in all kinds of businesses. My first job actually in Montreal was a delivery boy of flowers, bringing flowers from door to door. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can explain this in two ways. Number one, when I was in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was in Wurzburg, as I said, I gave this Spanish sample class. And she was there. I saw her, but I didn't know very much about her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I met her again when we came to New York. In 1948, 1947, I came, I came to New York. And my wife had relatives there whom I knew. Because these relatives were related somehow to the people who had the house in Bremen, you know? And so I met them. So I met them through them. And we got married in 1948. And you had children? Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, we have three children. We have a boy, David, who lives in Monsey now. He has a family. He is an actuary. We have a daughter who lives in Cleveland, Ohio. She's married. She was a journalist. Now she is a teacher. And we have a younger daughter, Judy, who lives here, who will come over now. And she is a mathematician and stastician. My wife also is biologist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trudel, T-R-U-D-E-L, second name, nee Farntrog. That's a German name-- F-A-R-N-T-R-O-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And over the years, have you talked a lot about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have not only talked about it, I have written about it. I have put it into form for children, especially 10 to 12-year-old children, 13-years children, to different newspapers, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes I'm asked to talk in their day schools or in colleges, American Catholic college, you see? What is the name of it, the Catholic college here, you see, or the American university, and to tell them about this. They want to know how the Jews lived during that time, growing up in Nazi Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I'm doing this for the museum at times. Now that it's 60 years later, you know, thinking back, is there a message you would like to convey to future generations and the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said the worst thing in the world is that people hate each other. And I said it once when a teacher in my class in Germany gave a speech to us. And he said to a student, everyone who has detached ears, stand up or sit down. Because this is Aryan. Anyone who doesn't have a crooked nose, sit down. In the end, nearly everyone sat down. I sat down, too. And he was surprised that I sat down. And I would be an Arya-- Aryan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, when the world was created, he said, God created the world. And they speak about the Darwin theory, Darwin's theory, Darwin's theory, the survival of the fittest, see? And the fittest, he said. The Nazis are the fittest. The Germans are the fittest. Already from olden times, from the Teutonic times 2,000 years ago, we are the fittest. And we exist because we have this fuhrer principle. That's why he called himself fuhrer, leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anyone else who does not live up and cannot survive has to be exterminated. Because it is a fight between the superior and the inferior. And we are the superior, the teacher told us, and different other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to the teacher, there isn't such a thing as superior or inferior in this world. Every person both created. And every person has a certain function, even if he's disabled. And I said, don't say ever survive superior and inferior. Say different. There are different people. Some people can do this. some people can do this. Everyone has a function. And they supplement each other, complement each other. So, so, so therefore, I, I got along with people, you see? And

speaker: interviewee
text: It makes no difference if they are what they call minority and, and, and, and majority, minority, and majority. It doesn't make a difference, minority and majority. They are all the same. You cannot say because I'm this and I'm that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are different. And they give different things to society. Everyone gives something to society. And sometimes they are up. Sometimes they are low, like we. Sometimes we feel good. Sometimes we don't feel so good. So their only difference is between that we are different, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that doesn't mean that we should hate each other. Because we can get things from them. And they can get things on them, the exchange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that we haven't talked about that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned to you about the class and the things which happened in school and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that people were treated there, you see, differently, you see? We don't treat everyone alike, you know? And sometimes we may not like everything what that other person does. But that doesn't mean to hate them and to do things of this sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because to other people and tell them all the time that we live together, you see? And sometimes you feel good, sometimes we're not good. But we can all contribute. And they all contribute. They don't know-- shouldn't consider themselves inferior or superior. We do everything what we can at all times. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had something else. But I forgot about that. [LAUGHS] Because I can't remember everything, you see? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] So that's why you asked me the question once, that I got along with them, even with Parchman, you see? Because you have to be on the person's wavelength, you see? If you, if you, if you antagonize him, you see, you won't get anything. But one thing is it, you see? Even when I went to school, I can argue with them. And I can-- but that doesn't mean I can hate them. But I will not give up my principles, you see? They asked me, for instance, why don't you give up your religion? Why don't you give up this? Way don't you give up this? I don't have to give it up. Even if I keep it, I can still talk to you. But that doesn't mean to hate you. And unfortunately, we have a lot of hate in this world. But talking alone is not the thing. You have to feel this way. Just doing it is not enough. You have to act this way. You cannot they say, there's a commandment not to kill, not to steal, you see? So I don't want to kill and steal. I have it in my mind. But when it comes to action, to doing it, that's what counts.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope everyone will be well. We won't have this again, such a Holocaust. This was the worst Holocaust in years, in the history of the people of humankind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a picture of my mother's family, taken about the end of the last century-- 1896, I would say. And in the background you have, from left to right, on the left hand Louis, L-O-U-I-S, Stiefel, in the middle, Martha, M-A-R-T-H-A, the other end, Josef, J-O-S-E-F. The front line, there is, left, Angela. In the middle is my mother, who was the youngest. And on the other side is Jenny, J-E-N-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were your mother's brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my mother's family, six people in her family. This picture is a picture of my father's family. Standing to your outer right is my father with a cap. Sitting in front are his parents-- his father, Abraham, and his mother, Recha. The other people, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been taken when he was in his teens, about the beginning of this century. I would say about 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a picture of the four children in my family. From left to right, I'm on the left side. Next to me is my sister, Liesl. Next is my brother, Benno. And then there is my younger brother, Alfred.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the class, the final class in the Wurzburg of teacher seminary in 1938 with the classmates. And I'm sitting there in the second lane, the utter right. This picture is a picture of my mother which was duplicated or copied from a small passport picture which was made in the first week, one week before the Kristallnacht, in November, 1938. It was a picture which I found after I came back from the prison in Wurzburg from the protective custody in Wurzburg. These are two pages from the passport, my stateless passport, made out in German and French. And it was made just before I left Germany. And I signed my name still Zwienicki in a different form than the printed name of Zwienicki. This is a picture of my wife and me around the time when we got married in 1948. It must have been in March or April, 1940-- 1938. No, 1948, excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taking in New York, I think in the Bronx. Mention the names of the people or not?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you can just, mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son's family, who-- they live in Monsey, New York. And it was taken about 1992, 1993. And you see here him and his wife and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eight children. If you start standing in the back, to the left is his oldest son, Shlomo. Next to him is my son, David. Second line at the left, his son, Josef, his daughter, Gila, the baby, Mordechai, and his wife, Trudy, Trudel, Trudy, and his daughter, [? Elisa. ?] In front is a boy, Yehuda, and the girl, Miriam Golda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Golda Miriam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Golda Miriam. and Eliezer. This is a picture of my daughter in Cleveland, Ohio. Standing in the back is the daughter, Adina. Next to her, my son-in-law, Joel. my daughter, Selma. In front, left is daughter, Rivka, next, Benjamin, Binyomin, next, Gershon, next, Chana, and in front of her, the youngest one, Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my younger daughter. In the back here, you see my wife, Trudel, and myself. Then you have, from left, David Stern, my daughter, Judy Stern, her daughter, Shira Stern, and in front, her son, Moshe Stern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Wiener, could you introduce the people who have joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This am I myself. This is Moshe. This is my wife, Trudel. In the back, we have Judy, my daughter Judy, and Shira, our granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy, how do you feel about your father doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's wonderful that he's doing this because it's very important for my daughter's generation, my children's generation, and all other people to know what happened in order that we can, can prevent something like this from happening again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shira, has your grandfather ever shared his story with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he talked to my school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trudel, is there anything you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd just like to say that hearing all the, the story again, all the troubles my husband [INAUDIBLE] had, and myself also to a lesser degree, I just have to think how good things turned out in the end and be grateful to the many people who helped us along the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all very much.
